channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Job Market Leaving Young Graduates Behind Could Scar Them For Years
item.description: The labor market has improved but people entering the work force are having a harder time starting careers, a dynamic that has had permanent effects in the past.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember

item.title: Inside Trump’s Strategy to Push Out Immigrants, and How Tech Giants Targeted Teens at School
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Nicholas Nehamas and Jennifer Valentino-DeVries
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump is using bureaucratic and regulatory changes to cut off both documented and undocumented immigrants from jobs and public services.

item.title: Deal to Renew FISA Surveillance Law Falters in the Senate Over Pulte
item.description: Democrats who had been expected to supply the votes necessary to advance it balked after President Trump named Bill Pulte to head the intelligence apparatus.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: Mr. Trump tapped Mr. Pulte, a businessman and close political ally who has used his role as federal housing director as a perch to carry out a campaign of retribution against the president’s perceived enemies.

item.title: House Passes Ukraine Aid in Defiance of Republican Leaders
item.description: Eighteen G.O.P. lawmakers broke with their party and joined Democrats to deliver yet another blow to the president’s foreign policy agenda.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: Ukrainian soldiers in the Donetsk region of eastern Ukraine in January. The House bill passed Thursday included $8 billion in loans to Ukraine and $1.8 billion in aid.

item.title: China Builds an Economic Fortress as Global Tensions Rise
item.description: Beijing says the changes are needed for national security, but they could complicate efforts by Chinese companies to find growth overseas.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:description: A robot made by a Chinese company displayed in April at an expo in Guangzhou, China. Beijing unveiled rules this week that would slow the overseas expansion of Chinese companies.

item.title: What To Know as China’s Xi Jinping Heads to North Korea
item.description: As Xi Jinping visits Pyongyang, he faces an emboldened North Korean dictator, whose alliance with Russia has reduced his dependence on China.
item.dc:creator: David Pierson and Choe Sang-Hun
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s leader, with North Korea’s leader, Kim Jong-un, in Beijing in September. Analysts say China is likely keen to assert its influence over North Korea as Pyongyang has leaned toward Russia.

item.title: Several Women Who Dated Graham Platner Recall ‘Unsettling’ Behavior
item.description: The Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine could be charming, women said in interviews, but some found his actions intimidating and disturbing.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck and Lisa Lerer
item.media:credit: Gabe Souza for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner, a combat veteran, has spoken openly about his past struggles with post-traumatic stress disorder, depression and drinking, and has said his behavior years ago does not reflect who he is today.

item.title: Platner Denies Hurting Ex-Girlfriend and Says He Will Not Quit Senate Race
item.description: In an interview after The Times reported on his treatment of women he had dated, Graham Platner acknowledged “not exactly acting with the best behavior” after his military service.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Graham Platner, the Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, said in an interview he would “absolutely take responsibility” for elements of his personal history.

item.title: Why Harlem May Be the Deciding Factor in a Critical House Race
item.description: Representative Adriano Espaillat and his progressive challenger, Darializa Avila Chevalier, have zeroed in on Harlem, traditionally hostile turf for the Democratic incumbent.
item.dc:creator: Sally Goldenberg and Maya King
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Adriano Espaillat held a campaign rally with labor leaders in Harlem last week, part of an effort to court voters in an area where his support is weaker.

item.title: Appeals Court Panel to Hear Arguments Over Trump’s Ballroom
item.description: A three-judge panel will consider whether a lower court erred in ordering construction stopped until President Trump secured the support of Congress.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: President Trump brought reporters to the site of his ballroom project in May, where construction has continued despite a pending lawsuit.

item.title: Trump Says Lee Greenwood and Christopher Macchio Will Replace Freedom 250 Artists
item.description: Artists including Young MC, Martina McBride and Bret Michaels had publicly dropped out of concerts scheduled for the National Mall.
item.dc:creator: Jason M. Bailey
item.media:description: Lee Greenwood performing last month. In his announcement, President Trump called Mr. Greenwood’s song “God Bless the U.S.A.” “one of the greatest hits of all time.”

item.title: Postal Service Frees Up Enough Cash to Keep Operating for ‘Several Years’
item.description: The agency had warned it could run out of money within months, but its regulators said suspending payments to a retirement fund had given officials more time to find a permanent solution.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Postal Service has raised prices and slowed delivery standards in recent years as it struggles to stanch declining mail volume and rising structural costs.

item.title: Hopes of Lebanon Cease-Fire Falter as Israel and Hezbollah Fight On
item.description: The U.S.-brokered agreement requires Hezbollah, which Iran backs, to stop firing first. But the group, not party to the talks, rejected the conditions as a virtual surrender.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smoke billowing from an Israeli strike near Nabatieh in southern Lebanon on Friday.

item.title: A Murdoch Builds His Own Media Empire. Is This ‘Succession’ or Secession?
item.description: James Murdoch says acquiring New York magazine and Vox has nothing to do with his father. But in some ways, it’s a tribute.
item.dc:creator: Jim Rutenberg

item.title: When Jeffrey Epstein Needed Favors, This Restaurant Mogul Was There
item.description: Stephen Hanson, whose empire included Blue Water Grill and Ruby Foo’s, was a devoted friend and wingman who helped manage and entertain the women in Epstein’s orbit.
item.dc:creator: Kim Severson

item.title: The Big Questions About Jeffrey Epstein: What The Times Has Learned
item.description: Here is what we know about the sexual predator whose secrets spurred an international reckoning over money, power and complicity. Check back for updates.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Former Prince Andrew Sublet Cottages on Royal Property Where He Lived Rent-Free
item.description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor received income from renting out three cottages at Royal Lodge, where he paid no rent himself, a British public spending watchdog found.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor lived at Royal Lodge, an estate in Windsor, until February, when he was ousted after new revelations emerged about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: How Gold Is Driving the Spread of Ebola
item.description: Mining has been the lifeblood of this remote Congolese hill town for decades. Now, it is fueling the spread of a devastating outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.category: Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: They Shut the Golden Gate Bridge for 4 Hours. Now They Face Up to 15 Years in Prison.
item.description: Activists blocked the iconic span to protest the spending of American tax dollars on Israeli military efforts in Gaza. San Francisco’s prosecutor is now seeking a strict punishment.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Lea Suzuki/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Pedestrians and bicyclists waited outside the pedestrian gate on the south side of the Golden Gate Bridge when the bridge was closed because of protesters in 2024.

item.title: Oakley Isn’t Barred From Madison Square Garden Anymore. Will He Come See the Knicks Play?
item.description: Charles Oakley, the 1990s Knicks star, hasn’t been to a game at Madison Square Garden in nearly a decade because of a legal battle.
item.dc:creator: Tania Ganguli and Santul Nerkar
item.media:description: Charles Oakley was briefly barred from Madison Square Garden in 2017 after he was removed from a game there after an altercation with security guards.

item.title: Backed by Trump, Opposed by Putin, and Fighting for His Political Life
item.description: Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan of Armenia is seeking re-election as his country weighs questions of war and peace, of autocracy and democracy, and of subjugation and independence.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Emile Ducke
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times

item.title: Russia Floods Armenia With Disinformation Ahead of Election
item.description: Fearing a loss of influence, groups linked to the Kremlin and intelligence agencies have sought to discredit the country’s prime minister.
item.dc:creator: Steven Lee Myers
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: A campaign event last week for Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan ahead of Armenian parliamentary elections.

item.title: Russia Rails Against the West but Welcomes Candace Owens and Andrew Tate
item.description: While some powerful Russians shun the West, others want to restore ties and embrace friendly Westerners. President Vladimir V. Putin’s annual economic conference illustrates the conflicting impulses.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: A screen with an image of President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia at the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum on Wednesday.

item.title: Zelensky Mixes Taunts and Peace Talks Offer in Letter to Putin
item.description: “After 26 years in power, age is beginning to take its toll,” President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine wrote of his Russian counterpart, bragging of a recent strike on St. Petersburg.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine, speaking in Sweden last week.

item.title: Europe Needs New Military Alliance to Defend Itself, Italy Says
item.description: Guido Crosetto, Italy’s defense minister, said in an interview that European nations should forge a new joint defense system and take greater responsibility for their own security.
item.dc:creator: Motoko Rich
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Italian marines during a military exercise in the Arctic Circle in 2024.

item.title: Ireland, Seen as a Weak Link in Europe’s Defense, Is Trying to Bulk Up
item.description: As concern rises in Europe over threats from an emboldened Russia, the Irish government says it’s working to plug gaps in its military, which reflect a tradition of neutrality.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia and Paulo Nunes dos Santos

item.title: In a First, Scientists Precisely Edit Human Embryo Genes
item.description: Researchers relied on a newer gene-editing technique that may make it possible to engineer embryos, a prospect that has long alarmed bioethicists.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer
item.media:description: Researchers altered genes that influence levels of LDL cholesterol and hemoglobin in the blood.

item.title: 38 Tony Nominees Reveal the Strangest Skills They’ve Picked Up
item.description: The stars of “Giant,” “Fallen Angels,” “The Rocky Horror Show,” “Ragtime” and more prove they’ll go to great lengths to be believable in a role.
item.dc:creator: Dana Scruggs, Michael Paulson, Alexis Soloski, Nicole Herrington, Jolie Ruben and Amanda Webster
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards Predictions: Who Will Win?
item.description: Expect wins for the musical “Schmigadoon!” and the play “Liberation,” and for the “Ragtime” stars Joshua Henry and Caissie Levy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)

item.title: Onstage and off
item.description: The Tony Awards are this weekend. We explore what the ceremony is all about.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.media:description: “Schmigadoon!”

item.title: The Betrayal of Black Voters Threatens Our Democracy
item.description: The sweeping effort to dismantle Black-majority congressional districts in the South will have far-reaching consequences for all Americans, and for our democracy.
item.dc:creator: Mara Gay

item.title: Anna Paulina Luna Wants Everything Disclosed
item.description: Anna Paulina Luna on Epstein, J.F.K. and U.F.O.s.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Victoria Chamberlin
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Late Night Sizes Up Trump’s Reflecting-Pool Ambitions
item.description: “One thing we know for sure is that Trump is definitely obsessed with size,” Seth Meyers said of the president’s bragging about the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: James Handy, Actor in ‘Top Gun: Maverick,’ Is Found Stabbed to Death
item.description: The 81-year-old actor had parts in films including “Top Gun: Maverick” and “Arachnophobia.” The son of his girlfriend was arrested and charged with murder.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: Adam Larkey/Disney General Entertainment Content, via Getty Images
item.media:description: James Handy in a 2010 episode of the television show “Castle.”
